mn58:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Rājagaha, in the Bamboo Grove, the squirrels’ feeding ground.
mn58:10.1: “Then, prince, I will question you in return about this very matter. Answer it as it seems right to you.
mn58:10.10: All the parts of a chariot are well known to me.
mn58:10.11: It would occur to me on the spot.”
mn58:10.3: are you skilled in the parts of a chariot?”
mn58:10.4: “Yes, bhante, I am skilled in the parts of a chariot.”
mn58:10.6: if people were to approach you and ask thus:
mn58:10.7: ‘What is the name of this part of a chariot?’
mn58:10.8: would this already have been thought out beforehand in your mind: ‘Those who approach me and ask thus, when asked thus I will answer thus’? Or would it occur to you on the spot?”
mn58:10.9: “For I, bhante, am known as a charioteer skilled in the parts of a chariot.
mn58:11.1: “In the same way, prince, when those wise nobles, wise brahmins, wise householders, or wise ascetics prepare a question and approach the Tathāgata to ask it, it occurs to the Tathāgata on the spot.
mn58:11.3: For, prince, the Tathāgata has fully penetrated the *dhamma* element [*dhammadhātu*]. Because that *dhamma* element [*dhammadhātuyā*] has been fully penetrated, it occurs to the Tathāgata on the spot.”
mn58:12.1: When this was said, Prince Abhaya said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn58:12.3: From today onward, may the *Bhagavā* remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.”
mn58:12.4: The eighth, Prince Abhaya, is finished.
mn58:2.1: Then Prince Abhaya went to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta. Having gone to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta and paid homage to him, he sat down to one side. As Prince Abhaya was sitting to one side, Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta said this to him:
mn58:3.1: “Come, prince, raise an argument against the ascetic Gotama.
mn58:3.10: For an ordinary person too would say words that are unlikable and disagreeable to others.’
mn58:3.11: But if, when asked thus, the ascetic Gotama answers thus:
mn58:3.12: ‘No, prince, the Tathāgata would not say words that are unlikable and disagreeable to others,’ then you should say to him:
mn58:3.13: ‘Then why, bhante, did you declare of Devadatta:
mn58:3.14: “Devadatta is bound for the bad destination, Devadatta is bound for hell, Devadatta will remain there for an aeon, Devadatta is beyond remedy”?
mn58:3.15: And because of those words of yours, Devadatta became angry and upset.’
mn58:3.16: When asked this two-pronged question, the ascetic Gotama will be unable either to spit it out or swallow it down.
mn58:3.17: Just as if an iron spike were stuck in a man’s throat, and he could neither spit it out nor swallow it down;
mn58:3.18: so too, prince, when asked this two-pronged question, the ascetic Gotama will be unable either to spit it out or swallow it down.”
mn58:3.2: Then a good report of your reputation will spread abroad:
mn58:3.3: ‘Prince Abhaya has raised an argument against the ascetic Gotama, so powerful and so mighty.’”
mn58:3.4: “But how, bhante, could I raise an argument against the ascetic Gotama, so powerful and so mighty?”
mn58:3.5: “Come, prince, go to the ascetic Gotama. Having gone to the ascetic Gotama, say to him:
mn58:3.6: ‘Would the Tathāgata, bhante, say words that are unlikable and disagreeable to others?’
mn58:3.7: If, when asked thus, the ascetic Gotama answers thus:
mn58:3.8: ‘The Tathāgata, prince, would say words that are unlikable and disagreeable to others,’ then you should say to him:
mn58:3.9: ‘Then what difference is there between you, bhante, and an ordinary person?
mn58:4.1: “Yes, bhante,” Prince Abhaya replied to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta. Then he rose from his seat, paid homage to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, and keeping him on his right, went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone to the *Bhagavā* and paid homage to him, he sat down to one side.
mn58:4.2: As Prince Abhaya was sitting to one side, he looked at the sun and thought:
mn58:4.3: “Today is not the right time to raise an argument against the *Bhagavā*.
mn58:4.4: Tomorrow at my own residence I will raise an argument against the *Bhagavā*.” He said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn58:4.5: “May the *Bhagavā*, bhante, consent to tomorrow’s meal for four, including yourself.”
mn58:5.1: Then, knowing that the *Bhagavā* had consented, Prince Abhaya rose from his seat, paid homage to the *Bhagavā*, and keeping him on his right, departed.
mn58:5.2: Then, when that night had passed, in the morning the *Bhagavā* robed himself and, taking bowl and robe, went to Prince Abhaya’s residence. Having gone there, he sat down on the seat made ready.
mn58:5.3: Then Prince Abhaya personally served and satisfied the *Bhagavā* with exquisite solid and soft foods.
mn58:5.4: Then, when the *Bhagavā* had eaten and had withdrawn his hand from the bowl, Prince Abhaya took a certain low seat and sat down to one side.
mn58:6.1: As he was sitting to one side, Prince Abhaya said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn58:6.10: “Prince Abhaya has raised an argument against the ascetic Gotama, so powerful and so mighty.”
mn58:6.11: When this was said, bhante, I said this to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta:
mn58:6.12: ‘But how, bhante, could I raise an argument against the ascetic Gotama, so powerful and so mighty?’
mn58:6.13: ‘Come, prince, go to the ascetic Gotama. Having gone to the ascetic Gotama, say to him:
mn58:6.14: “Would the Tathāgata, bhante, say words that are unlikable and disagreeable to others?”
mn58:6.15: If, when asked thus, the ascetic Gotama answers thus:
mn58:6.16: “The Tathāgata, prince, would say words that are unlikable and disagreeable to others,” then you should say to him:
mn58:6.17: “Then what difference is there between you, bhante, and an ordinary person?
mn58:6.18: For an ordinary person too would say words that are unlikable and disagreeable to others.”
mn58:6.19: But if, when asked thus, the ascetic Gotama answers thus:
mn58:6.2: “Would the Tathāgata, bhante, say words that are unlikable and disagreeable to others?”
mn58:6.20: “No, prince, the Tathāgata would not say words that are unlikable and disagreeable to others,” then you should say to him—
mn58:6.21: then why, bhante, did you declare of Devadatta:
mn58:6.22: “Devadatta is bound for the bad destination, Devadatta is bound for hell, Devadatta will remain there for an aeon, Devadatta is beyond remedy”?
mn58:6.23: And because of those words of yours, Devadatta became angry and upset.’
mn58:6.24: When asked this two-pronged question, the ascetic Gotama will be unable either to spit it out or swallow it down.
mn58:6.25: Just as if an iron spike were stuck in a man’s throat, and he could neither spit it out nor swallow it down;
mn58:6.26: so too, prince, when asked this two-pronged question, the ascetic Gotama will be unable either to spit it out or swallow it down.’”
mn58:6.3: “There is no one-sided answer to that, prince.”
mn58:6.4: “In this, bhante, the Nigaṇṭhas have failed.”
mn58:6.5: “But why do you say this, prince:
mn58:6.6: ‘In this, bhante, the Nigaṇṭhas have failed’?”
mn58:6.7: “Here, bhante, I went to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta. Having gone to Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta and paid homage to him, I sat down to one side. As I was sitting to one side, bhante, Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta said this to me:
mn58:6.8: ‘Come, prince, raise an argument against the ascetic Gotama.
mn58:6.9: Then a good report of your reputation will spread abroad—
mn58:7.1: Now at that time a little infant prince, lying on his back, was sitting on Prince Abhaya’s lap.
mn58:7.2: Then the *Bhagavā* said this to Prince Abhaya:
mn58:7.4: if, through your carelessness or through the nurse’s carelessness, this boy were to put a stick or a potsherd into his mouth, what would you do to him?”
mn58:7.6: If, bhante, I could not take it out immediately, then holding his head with my left hand and hooking a finger of my right hand, I would take it out even if it drew blood.
mn58:7.8: Because, bhante, I have compassion for the boy.”
mn58:8.1: “In the same way, prince, any words that the Tathāgata knows to be untrue, unfactual, and not beneficial, and also unlikable and disagreeable to others—the Tathāgata does not say such words.
mn58:8.2: And any words that the Tathāgata knows to be true, factual, but not beneficial, and also unlikable and disagreeable to others—the Tathāgata does not say such words.
mn58:8.3: But any words that the Tathāgata knows to be true, factual, and beneficial, though unlikable and disagreeable to others, regarding them the Tathāgata knows the right time for saying them.
mn58:8.4: Any words that the Tathāgata knows to be untrue, unfactual, and not beneficial, though likable and agreeable to others—the Tathāgata does not say such words.
mn58:8.5: And any words that the Tathāgata knows to be true, factual, but not beneficial, though likable and agreeable to others, the Tathāgata does not say such words.
mn58:8.6: But any words that the Tathāgata knows to be true, factual, and beneficial, and also likable and agreeable to others, regarding them the Tathāgata knows the right time for saying them.
mn58:8.8: Because, prince, the Tathāgata has compassion for beings.”
mn58:9.1: “Bhante, when these wise nobles, wise brahmins, wise householders, or wise ascetics prepare a question and approach the Tathāgata to ask it,
mn58:9.2: has this already been thought out beforehand in the *Bhagavā*’s mind: ‘Those who approach me and ask thus, when asked thus I will answer thus’? Or does it occur to the Tathāgata on the spot?”
